title: Limits of combined functions: sums and differences
description: Determine limits of a sum (or difference) of two functions whose graphs are given.
topic_path: Determining limits using algebraic properties of limits: limit properties
Limits of combined functions: sums and differences

assessment_items.question_content:
Functions $f$ and $g$ are graphed.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 4 to 4. The graph consists of a set of line segments. The set starts at (negative 4, 4), moves horizontally to the right to (negative 2, 4), moves downward to (2, 0), moves upward and ends at (4, 4).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/04fa425e84a2c87665cf40b95ec14e09639bfba8) ![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 4 to 4. The graph consists of a set of line segments. The set starts at (negative 4, negative 2), moves upward to (negative 1, 4), moves downward to (0, 1), moves upward through an open circle at (2, 3), and ends at (4, 5).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5790a7c07d11038045950922dfb4d2f7fc47f8f4)

**Find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}\Big(f(x)-g(x)\Big)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Putting it all together:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}\Big(f(x)-g(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=\blueD{\lim_{x\to 2}f(x)}-\maroonD{\lim_{x\to 2}g(x)}
\\\\
&=\blueD 0-\maroonD 3
\\\\
&=-3
\end{align}$

**Find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}\Big(h(x)+g(x)\Big)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Putting it all together:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}\Big(h(x)+g(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=\blueD{\lim_{x\to -3}h(x)}+\maroonD{\lim_{x\to -3}g(x)}
\\\\
&=\blueD 3+\maroonD 2
\\\\
&=5
\end{align}$

**Find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\Big(g(x)-h(x)\Big)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Putting it all together:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\Big(g(x)-h(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=\blueD{\lim_{x\to 0}g(x)}-\maroonD{\lim_{x\to 0}h(x)}
\\\\
&=\blueD {-1}-\maroonD 0
\\\\
&=-1
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Function $f$ is graphed.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 4 to 4. The graph consists of a set of line segments and a closed circle. The set starts at (negative 4, 1), moves downward to (negative 2, negative 1), moves upward to (0, 3), moves downward to (1, 2), moves horizontally to the right through an open circle at (3, 2), and ends at (4, 2). The closed circle is plotted at (3, 4).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f64e067aea97f8a08acfa02ebe23587a0a880ee1)

**Find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}\Big(5f(x)\Big)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
According to the graph, $\blueD{\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}f(x)=2}$.

Now we can find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}\Big(5f(x)\Big)$ using the fact that the limit of a multiple of a function is the multiple of the limit:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}\Big(5f(x)\Big)=5\cdot \lim_{x\to 3}f(x)$

Putting it all together:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}\Big(5f(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=5\cdot\blueD{\lim_{x\to 3}f(x)}
\\\\
&=5\cdot\blueD 2
\\\\
&=10
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Function $g$ is graphed.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 4 to 4. The graph consists of a set of line segments. The set starts at (negative 4, 4), moves upward to (negative 3, 5), moves downward to (negative 1, 3), moves horizontally to the right through an open circle at (0, 3) to (1, 3), moves downward and ends at (4, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d89d3578129ad68264e30be2bef8a255fdf0ff79)

**Find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\Big(-2g(x)\Big)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
According to the graph, $\blueD{\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}g(x)=3}$.

Now we can find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\Big(-2g(x)\Big)$ using the fact that the limit of a multiple of a function is the multiple of the limit:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\Big(-2g(x)\Big)=-2\cdot \lim_{x\to 0}g(x)$

Putting it all together:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\Big(-2g(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=-2\cdot\blueD{\lim_{x\to 0}g(x)}
\\\\
&=-2\cdot\blueD 3
\\\\
&=-6
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Function $h$ is graphed.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 4 to 4. The graph consists of a set of line segments. The set starts at (negative 4, 5), moves downward through (negative 3, 4) to (negative 1, 2), moves horizontally to the right to (1, 2),  moves upward to (negative 2, 3), moves downward and ends at (4, negative 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/09de0b3c8593c167d8478c53bb20f1ea1d0ef7f2)

**Find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}\Big(0.5h(x)\Big)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
According to the graph, $\blueD{\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}h(x)=4}$.

Now we can find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}\Big(0.5h(x)\Big)$ using the fact that the limit of a multiple of a function is the multiple of the limit:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}\Big(0.5h(x)\Big)=0.5\cdot \lim_{x\to -3}h(x)$

Putting it all together:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}\Big(0.5h(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=0.5\cdot\blueD{\lim_{x\to -3}h(x)}
\\\\
&=0.5\cdot\blueD 4
\\\\
&=2
\end{align}$

**Find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}\Big(h(x)-g(x)\Big)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's find the limit from the left:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^-}\Big(h(x)-g(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=\blueD{\lim_{x\to 3^-}h(x)}-\maroonD{\lim_{x\to 3^-}g(x)}
\\\\
&=\blueD 2-\maroonD 2
\\\\
&=0
\end{align}$

Now let's find the limit from the right:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^+}\Big(h(x)-g(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=\purpleD{\lim_{x\to 3^+}h(x)}-\maroonD{\lim_{x\to 3^+}g(x)}
\\\\
&=\purpleD 5-\maroonD 2
\\\\
&=3
\end{align}$

The one-sided limits are different and therefore the limit doesn't exist.

**Find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}\Big(f(x)+h(x)\Big)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's find the limit from the left:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3^-}\Big(f(x)+h(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=\blueD{\lim_{x\to -3^-}f(x)}+\maroonD{\lim_{x\to -3^-}h(x)}
\\\\
&=\blueD 3+\maroonD 2
\\\\
&=5
\end{align}$

Now let's find the limit from the right:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3^+}\Big(f(x)+h(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=\purpleD{\lim_{x\to -3^+}f(x)}+\maroonD{\lim_{x\to -3^+}h(x)}
\\\\
&=\purpleD 0+\maroonD 2
\\\\
&=2
\end{align}$

The one-sided limits are different and therefore the limit doesn't exist.

**Find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\Big(2g(x)\Big)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's find the limit from the left:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^-}\Big(2g(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=2\cdot\blueD{\lim_{x\to 0^-}g(x)}
\\\\
&=2\cdot\blueD 1
\\\\
&=2
\end{align}$

Now let's find the limit from the right:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^+}\Big(2g(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=2\cdot\purpleD{\lim_{x\to 0^+}g(x)}
\\\\
&=2\cdot\purpleD 3
\\\\
&=6
\end{align}$

The one-sided limits are different and therefore the limit doesn't exist.

The second set starts at a closed circle at (1, 3), moves horizontally to the right to (2, 3), moves upward and ends at (4, 4).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/590255de1f36c472e6c417aeecece9ba3eff3eeb)

**Find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1}\Big(f(x)+g(x)\Big)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's find the limit from the left:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^-}\Big(f(x)+g(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=\blueD{\lim_{x\to 1^-}f(x)}+\maroonD{\lim_{x\to 1^-}g(x)}
\\\\
&=\blueD 3+\maroonD 1
\\\\
&=4
\end{align}$

Now let's find the limit from the right:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^+}\Big(f(x)+g(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=\purpleD{\lim_{x\to 1^+}f(x)}+\goldD{\lim_{x\to 1^+}g(x)}
\\\\
&=\purpleD 1+\goldD 3
\\\\
&=4
\end{align}$

The one-sided limits are both equal to $4$ and therefore $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1}\Big(f(x)+g(x)\Big)=4$.

**Find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}\Big(f(x)-h(x)\Big)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's find the limit from the left:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^-}\Big(f(x)-h(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=\blueD{\lim_{x\to -1^-}f(x)}-\maroonD{\lim_{x\to -1^-}h(x)}
\\\\
&=\blueD 2-\maroonD 1
\\\\
&=1
\end{align}$

Now let's find the limit from the right:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^+}\Big(f(x)-h(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=\purpleD{\lim_{x\to -1^+}f(x)}-\goldD{\lim_{x\to -1^+}h(x)}
\\\\
&=\purpleD 1-\goldD 0
\\\\
&=1
\end{align}$

The one-sided limits are both equal to $1$ and therefore $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}\Big(f(x)-h(x)\Big)=1$.

**Find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}\Big(g(x)+h(x)\Big)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's find the limit from the left:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^-}\Big(g(x)+h(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=\blueD{\lim_{x\to -2^-}g(x)}+\maroonD{\lim_{x\to -2^-}h(x)}
\\\\
&=\blueD 1+\maroonD 4
\\\\
&=5
\end{align}$

Now let's find the limit from the right:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^+}\Big(g(x)+h(x)\Big)
\\\\
&=\purpleD{\lim_{x\to -2^+}g(x)}+\goldD{\lim_{x\to -2^+}h(x)}
\\\\
&=\purpleD 4+\goldD 1
\\\\
&=5
\end{align}$

The one-sided limits are both equal to $5$ and therefore $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}\Big(g(x)+h(x)\Big)=5$.
